QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 11 — Right to access information. A data principal can obtain a summary of their personal data being processed and the processing activities carried out.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We have a documented process for correction, completion, updating, and erasure of personal data on request.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Set up an intake form and SLA tracker for correction/erasure requests.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 12 — Right to correction and erasure. A data principal can request correction of inaccurate/misleading data, completion of incomplete data, updating, and erasure of data no longer necessary for its purpose.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We support nomination of another individual to exercise a data principal's rights in the event of death or incapacity.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add a nominee-designation field and a documented handling process for nominee requests.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 14 — Right to nominate. A data principal can nominate another individual to exercise their rights in the event of death or incapacity.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Data principals are made aware of their duties under the Act (providing accurate information, not impersonating others, not filing false or frivolous grievances).
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add a short 'your responsibilities' section alongside the privacy notice covering data principal duties.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 15 — Duties of data principals. Data principals also have duties — not to impersonate, not to suppress material information, not to register false or frivolous grievances, and to provide only authentic information.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We obtain verifiable parental or guardian consent before processing a child's personal data.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Implement age-gating and a verifiable parental consent workflow for any user under 18.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We do not undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Disable behavioural tracking and ad targeting for accounts flagged or verified as belonging to children.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: For persons with disabilities who have a lawful guardian, we process personal data through the consent of that lawful guardian.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add a guardian-consent workflow for accounts linked to a lawful guardian.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: If classified (or likely to be classified) as a Significant Data Fiduciary, we have appointed a Data Protection Officer based in India.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Designate an India-based DPO and publish their contact details.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We have appointed, or would appoint, an independent data auditor to carry out periodic compliance audits.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Engage an independent auditor for periodic data protection compliance audits.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We conduct periodic Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Build a DPIA template and schedule periodic reassessment of high-risk processing activities.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We perform due diligence on algorithms/software used for processing to ensure they don't risk data principal rights.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add an algorithmic risk review step before deploying automated processing tools.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We monitor the Central Government's notified list of countries or territories where transfer of personal data is restricted.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Set up a periodic check against the government's notified restricted-country list before enabling new data flows.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(1) — A consent request must be presented in clear and plain language, and separately from any other unrelated information — no bundling or pre-ticked boxes.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Where another law provides a higher degree of protection or restriction on cross-border transfer, we comply with that stricter standard too.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Cross-check sector-specific laws (e.g. RBI data storage rules) alongside the DPDP Act's general transfer rule.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(2) — Where consent is given by electronic means, the Data Fiduciary must also offer a Consent Manager option, and consent must be as easily withdrawable as it was given.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We maintain a documented map of exactly which countries our vendors/cloud providers physically store or process personal data in.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Request data residency documentation from every vendor and maintain a central map of it.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: This reflects sound data-governance practice consistent with the Act's overall obligations, even where no single section names it explicitly.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: In the event of a personal data breach, we can intimate the Data Protection Board and each affected data principal as required.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Build a pre-drafted breach notification template for both the Board and affected individuals.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(6) — On a personal data breach, the Data Fiduciary must intimate the Data Protection Board and each affected data principal, in the manner prescribed.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We have a documented breach response plan with defined roles and escalation steps.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Draft an incident response runbook naming owners for detection, containment, and notification.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(6) — On a personal data breach, the Data Fiduciary must intimate the Data Protection Board and each affected data principal, in the manner prescribed.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We have a process for responding to inquiries or directions issued by the Data Protection Board.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Assign an internal owner responsible for coordinating any Data Protection Board correspondence.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Chapter V — The Data Protection Board of India. Establishes the Board as the body that hears grievances, directs remedial action, and imposes penalties for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Leadership is aware of the financial penalty exposure under the Act (up to ₹250 crore for breach-related failures) and factors it into risk planning.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add DPDP penalty exposure as a line item in the enterprise risk register.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: The Schedule — Penalties. Sets maximum financial penalties for various breaches, up to ₹250 crore for failing to take reasonable security safeguards or notify a breach, and up to ₹200 crore for violating children's data obligations.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We assess breach severity and reporting necessity using defined internal criteria, not ad hoc judgment calls.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Define objective breach-severity criteria in advance to remove ambiguity during an actual incident.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(6) — On a personal data breach, the Data Fiduciary must intimate the Data Protection Board and each affected data principal, in the manner prescribed.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Our breach notification to affected data principals uses clear, plain language explaining what happened and what they should do.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Draft a plain-language breach notice template covering what happened, impact, and recommended user action.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(6) — On a personal data breach, the Data Fiduciary must intimate the Data Protection Board and each affected data principal, in the manner prescribed.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We track Data Protection Board orders, penalties, and enforcement trends relevant to our sector to inform our risk posture.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Assign someone to monitor Data Protection Board enforcement actions relevant to your industry.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Chapter V — The Data Protection Board of India. Establishes the Board as the body that hears grievances, directs remedial action, and imposes penalties for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Where we rely on 'legitimate uses' instead of consent (e.g. voluntarily provided data, employment purposes, medical emergencies), we've documented which specific ground applies and why.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Map every non-consent processing activity to a specific Sec 7 legitimate use ground and record the justification.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 7 — Certain legitimate uses. Lists specific situations where personal data can be processed without consent — e.g. data voluntarily provided by the principal, state functions and benefits, medical emergencies, and employment purposes.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Consent artefacts are retained as evidence, and we can demonstrate when and how consent was obtained for any data principal.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Log timestamped consent records (what was shown, what was agreed) retrievable per data principal.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(2) — Where consent is given by electronic means, the Data Fiduciary must also offer a Consent Manager option, and consent must be as easily withdrawable as it was given.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Where we use a Consent Manager, it is registered with the Data Protection Board and operates on an interoperable platform.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Verify any third-party Consent Manager you route consent through is Board-registered before relying on it.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(7)–(9) — Consent may be managed through a Consent Manager, a Board-registered, interoperable platform that lets a principal give, manage, review, and withdraw consent through one interface.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Notices are available in languages required under the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution where relevant to our user base.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Localize the privacy notice into the relevant Eighth Schedule languages for your user base.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 5(3) — Notice must be available in English or any language listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution, per rules to be prescribed.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We ensure our data processors are engaged only under a valid contract before they process any personal data on our behalf.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Issue a valid processing contract to every processor before granting them access to personal data.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(2) — A Data Fiduciary may only engage a Data Processor under a valid contract governing that processing.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We remain responsible for compliance with the Act even where processing is carried out by a processor or under a data-sharing agreement.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Clarify in policy that fiduciary responsibility isn't outsourced merely by engaging a processor.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(1) — The Data Fiduciary remains responsible for compliance with the Act, regardless of any contract or arrangement with a processor.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure effective observance of the Act's provisions, not just security.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Extend your compliance program beyond security controls to cover process, documentation, and training measures too.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(4) — The Data Fiduciary must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to give effect to the Act's provisions, not just security controls.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Retention periods factor in both the stated purpose and any applicable legal requirement to retain data longer.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Cross-check retention schedules against sector-specific legal retention mandates before defaulting to purpose-based deletion.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 8(7)–(8) — Personal data must be erased once the specified purpose is no longer being served and retention isn't required by any other law, subject to any prescribed retention periods.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We do not require excessive documentation or fees that would discourage a data principal from exercising their rights.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Keep the rights-request process free or low-cost and proportionate, not a barrier to exercising rights.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 11 — Right to access information. A data principal can obtain a summary of their personal data being processed and the processing activities carried out.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We maintain records of rights requests received and their resolution status for audit purposes.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Log every rights request with timestamps and resolution status in a central tracker.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 13 — Right to grievance redressal. A Data Fiduciary must provide a readily available means for a data principal to register a grievance, and respond within a prescribed time before the principal can escalate to the Board.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We distinguish requests that are manifestly frivolous or excessive from legitimate ones, with a documented basis for any refusal.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Document objective criteria for identifying a frivolous/excessive request before declining to act on one.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 15 — Duties of data principals. Data principals also have duties — not to impersonate, not to suppress material information, not to register false or frivolous grievances, and to provide only authentic information.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We inform data principals of the identity and contact details of the Data Fiduciary responsible for processing their data.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Ensure your notice clearly names the accountable Data Fiduciary entity and its contact point.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 5(1)(a) — The notice must clearly identify the Data Fiduciary and provide contact details for questions about the processing.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We have a mechanism to detect or verify user age at signup where our service processes children's data at scale.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add an age-verification step at signup for services likely to be used by children.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We do not process children's data in any manner likely to cause a detrimental effect on their wellbeing.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Review product features (e.g. engagement/notification design) for detrimental effects on child wellbeing before launch.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We understand the government's criteria for Significant Data Fiduciary classification (data volume/sensitivity, risk to sovereignty, electoral democracy, security of the state, public order) and periodically reassess against them.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Reassess your SDF classification periodically as data volumes and processing activities grow.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10 — Additional obligations of Significant Data Fiduciary. The Central Government can classify certain fiduciaries as 'Significant' based on factors like data volume/sensitivity and risk to sovereignty or electoral integrity, triggering extra obligations.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Additional SDF measures (DPIA, audit, algorithmic due diligence) are reviewed and updated at least annually.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Put an annual review of all SDF-specific obligations on the compliance calendar.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Data localization or additional restrictions specific to Significant Data Fiduciaries have been checked against our cross-border data flows.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Cross-check any SDF-specific data localization requirement against your current cross-border transfer map.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 10(2) — A Significant Data Fiduciary must appoint a Data Protection Officer based in India, an independent data auditor, and undertake periodic DPIAs and compliance audits.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We've confirmed whether the destination country could appear on a future Central Government restriction notification before finalizing new vendor contracts.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Add a contractual clause allowing suspension of transfer if the destination country later becomes restricted.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(1) — A consent request must be presented in clear and plain language, and separately from any other unrelated information — no bundling or pre-ticked boxes.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We track evolving sectoral data localization mandates (e.g. RBI, insurance, telecom) that might override the DPDP Act's general cross-border rule.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Assign an owner to track sector regulator updates on data localization requirements.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: Section 6(2) — Where consent is given by electronic means, the Data Fiduciary must also offer a Consent Manager option, and consent must be as easily withdrawable as it was given.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: Cloud contracts specify the exact jurisdictions where backups and disaster-recovery copies of personal data are stored.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Extend your data residency map to cover backup and DR copies, not just primary storage.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: This reflects sound data-governance practice consistent with the Act's overall obligations, even where no single section names it explicitly.

QUESTIONS_DPDP.text: We reassess our cross-border transfer map whenever we onboard a new cloud region, vendor, or sub-processor.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.fix: Trigger a transfer-map review as a mandatory step in vendor/cloud-region onboarding.
QUESTIONS_DPDP.explain: This reflects sound data-governance practice consistent with the Act's overall obligations, even where no single section names it explicitly.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We have identified and documented a lawful basis for every processing purpose.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a data-processing inventory tagging each purpose with its lawful basis.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 6 — Lawfulness of processing. Every processing activity needs one of six lawful bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Special category data (health, biometric, sexual orientation, etc.) is processed only under one of the specific Article 9(2) conditions, with extra safeguards.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Tag special category data separately and document the specific Art 9(2) condition relied on for each use case.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 9 — Special categories of data. Processing data revealing racial/ethnic origin, health, biometric, genetic, sexual orientation, or similar sensitive attributes is prohibited unless a specific Article 9(2) exception applies.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Personal data is minimized, kept accurate, and not retained longer than necessary for its purpose.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Review data fields for necessity, add accuracy checks, and define retention limits per category.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 5(1)(c) — Data minimisation. Personal data collected must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what's necessary for the purpose — not gathered 'just in case.'

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We can demonstrate compliance with the Article 5 principles through documented policies and records (accountability).
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Maintain a documented accountability file showing how each Art 5 principle is met in practice.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 5(2) — Accountability. The controller must be able to demonstrate compliance with all the Article 5(1) principles, not just follow them silently.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We respond to access, rectification, erasure, and restriction requests within one month, as required.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Set up an intake form and SLA tracker guaranteeing a one-month response window.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 12 — Transparent information and modalities for rights. Controllers must respond to rights requests within one month, generally free of charge, using clear and plain language, and via the same channel the request came through.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We support the right to data portability in a structured, commonly-used, machine-readable format.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Add a machine-readable data export option (e.g. JSON/CSV) to account settings.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 20 — Right to data portability. Individuals can receive their data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and have it transmitted to another controller where technically feasible.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: When a data subject objects to processing, especially for direct marketing, we stop that processing immediately.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a marketing-objection flag that immediately suppresses further marketing processing.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 21 — Right to object. Individuals can object to processing based on legitimate interests or public task at any time; for direct marketing, the objection must be honoured immediately and unconditionally.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Where automated decision-making or profiling produces legal or similarly significant effects, we provide safeguards and a path to human review.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Disclose automated decision logic and offer a clear path to request human review.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 22 — Automated individual decision-making. Individuals have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing (including profiling) that has legal or similarly significant effects, with limited exceptions and required safeguards including human review.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We maintain Records of Processing Activities covering purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention, and security measures.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build and maintain a complete RoPA per the Art 30(1) required fields.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 30 — Records of processing activities. Controllers (and processors) must maintain a written record of their processing activities, describing purposes, categories of data and recipients, retention periods, and security measures.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Privacy by design and by default is built into new systems and products from the start, not bolted on afterward.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Add a privacy-by-design checklist to the product development lifecycle.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 25 — Data protection by design and by default. Privacy safeguards must be built into systems from the design stage, and default settings must be the most privacy-protective option.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Every processor we use is bound by a written contract covering all the mandatory Article 28(3) clauses.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Issue a standard DPA with all Art 28(3) clauses to every processor before onboarding.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 28 — Processor. Any processing carried out by a processor must be governed by a contract setting out the mandatory clauses in Article 28(3) (subject matter, duration, nature of processing, obligations, sub-processor rules, etc.).

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Where we act as a joint controller with another organization, the arrangement and respective responsibilities are documented.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Draft a joint-controller agreement documenting each party's responsibilities.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 26 — Joint controllers. Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they must have a transparent arrangement setting out each party's responsibilities.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We use pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Enforce TLS in transit and AES-256 at rest; pseudonymize data in lower environments.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 32(1)(a) — One of the specific measures Article 32 calls out: pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We can ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Deploy access controls, monitoring, and redundancy to protect system CIA properties.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 32(1)(b) — Another specific Article 32 measure: the ability to ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We can restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Schedule an annual restore drill to confirm backups and recovery actually work.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 32(1)(c) — Another specific Article 32 measure: the ability to restore availability and access to data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We regularly test, assess, and evaluate the effectiveness of our technical and organizational security measures.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Schedule quarterly vulnerability scans and an annual penetration test.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 32(1)(d) — Another specific Article 32 measure: a process for regularly testing, assessing, and evaluating the effectiveness of security measures.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We can notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach, unless it's unlikely to result in risk.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a pre-drafted regulator notification template so the 72-hour window is achievable.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 33 — Notification of a breach to the supervisory authority. A personal data breach must be notified to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of it, unless unlikely to result in risk to individuals — including specific required content and phased reporting where full details aren't yet available.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We communicate a breach to affected data subjects without undue delay when it's likely to result in high risk to them.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Create a pre-approved breach notification template for affected individuals.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 34 — Communication of a breach to the data subject. Where a breach is likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, the controller must notify those individuals directly, without undue delay.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We document every breach (facts, effects, remedial action taken) regardless of whether it required notification.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Log every incident, near-miss included, in a central breach register.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 33 — Notification of a breach to the supervisory authority. A personal data breach must be notified to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of it, unless unlikely to result in risk to individuals — including specific required content and phased reporting where full details aren't yet available.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We carry out a Data Protection Impact Assessment for processing likely to result in high risk to individuals.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a DPIA template and trigger it before launching any high-risk data feature.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 35 — Data protection impact assessment (DPIA). Where processing is likely to result in high risk, the controller must carry out a DPIA before starting, assessing the risk and mitigation measures.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We have appointed a Data Protection Officer where required (public authority, large-scale monitoring, or large-scale special category processing).
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Designate a DPO if your processing meets any Art 37(1) trigger condition.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 37 — Designation of a Data Protection Officer. A DPO is mandatory where the controller is a public authority, or its core activities involve large-scale regular monitoring, or large-scale processing of special category data.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Our DPO operates independently, reports to the highest level of management, and isn't instructed on how to carry out their tasks.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Formalize DPO independence and direct reporting line to senior leadership in writing.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 38 — Position of the DPO. The DPO must be involved properly and in a timely manner in all data protection matters, given the resources needed to perform their tasks, and act independently — reporting to the highest level of management.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: The DPO's contact details are published and communicated to the supervisory authority.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Publish DPO contact details externally and notify the relevant supervisory authority.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 37 — Designation of a Data Protection Officer. A DPO is mandatory where the controller is a public authority, or its core activities involve large-scale regular monitoring, or large-scale processing of special category data.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Transfers outside the EEA rely on an adequacy decision, Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules, or another valid Article 46 mechanism.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Map every international transfer to a specific, valid Art 46 transfer mechanism.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Chapter V (Articles 44–49) — Transfers of personal data to third countries. Any transfer of personal data outside the EEA needs a valid legal mechanism: an adequacy decision, appropriate safeguards (like Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules), or a specific derogation.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We conduct transfer impact assessments for SCC-based transfers, accounting for the destination country's surveillance laws.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Run a transfer impact assessment before relying on SCCs for higher-risk destination countries.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 46 — Transfers subject to appropriate safeguards. In the absence of an adequacy decision, transfers can rely on safeguards like Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules — but post-Schrems II, this also requires assessing whether the destination country's laws undermine those safeguards.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Article 49 derogations are used only as genuine, occasional exceptions, not as a routine transfer basis.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Restrict Art 49 derogation use to one-off exceptional transfers, not a standing transfer mechanism.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 49 — Derogations for specific situations. In the absence of an adequacy decision or safeguards, narrow exceptions (explicit consent, contract necessity, important public interest, etc.) allow transfers — but these are meant for occasional, not systematic, use.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We monitor for changes in adequacy decisions or SCC validity affecting our current transfer mechanisms.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Assign an owner to track adequacy decision changes and SCC updates relevant to your transfer routes.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Chapter V (Articles 44–49) — Transfers of personal data to third countries. Any transfer of personal data outside the EEA needs a valid legal mechanism: an adequacy decision, appropriate safeguards (like Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules), or a specific derogation.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Binding Corporate Rules, where used, have been formally approved by the competent supervisory authority.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Confirm BCRs are formally approved by a competent supervisory authority before relying on them as a transfer mechanism.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 47 — Binding Corporate Rules. BCRs let a corporate group transfer data internationally, but they must first be approved by the competent supervisory authority.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We maintain a record of all onward transfers by our processors to further sub-processors outside the EEA.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Require processors to disclose all further onward international transfers of the data they handle for you.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Chapter V (Articles 44–49) — Transfers of personal data to third countries. Any transfer of personal data outside the EEA needs a valid legal mechanism: an adequacy decision, appropriate safeguards (like Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules), or a specific derogation.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We rely on legitimate interest only after documenting a Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) balancing our interest against the individual's rights.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Document an LIA for every processing activity relying on legitimate interest as its lawful basis.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 6 — Lawfulness of processing. Every processing activity needs one of six lawful bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Personal data is processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security, integrity, and confidentiality.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Tie your security controls explicitly back to the Art 5(1)(f) integrity/confidentiality principle in your documentation.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 5(1)(f) — Integrity and confidentiality. Personal data must be processed with appropriate security, protecting against unauthorised access, loss, or damage.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We don't collect or retain extra identifying information just to be able to respond to data subject rights requests.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Avoid re-identifying anonymized/pseudonymized users solely to process a rights request unless they voluntarily provide extra info.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 11 — Processing not requiring identification. If a controller doesn't need to identify a data subject to achieve its purpose, it shouldn't collect extra identifying information solely to comply with the GDPR.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We process employee/HR data under a specific lawful basis and any applicable collective agreement or national employment-context rules.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Document the specific lawful basis and any collective agreement governing HR/employee data processing.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 88 — Processing in the employment context. Member states can adopt more specific rules for processing employees' data, which may include collective agreements — so HR data processing may have extra national/local requirements layered on top of the GDPR.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We can articulate a specific ground (e.g. purpose fulfilled, consent withdrawn, unlawful processing) before erasing data on request.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Map every erasure request to a specific Art 17(1) ground before acting on or refusing it.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 17 — Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). Individuals can request deletion of their data on specific grounds (e.g. purpose fulfilled, consent withdrawn, unlawful processing) — it isn't an unconditional right, so the controller must map the request to a specific ground.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Where we've disclosed personal data to third parties, we notify them of any rectification, erasure, or restriction, unless impossible or disproportionate.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a notify-recipients step into your rectification/erasure/restriction workflow.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 19 — Notification obligation regarding rectification, erasure, or restriction. Where data has been shared with others, the controller must tell those recipients about any correction, deletion, or restriction, unless that's impossible or disproportionate.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We provide rights-request responses free of charge, except for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Default to free rights-request handling; reserve fees only for manifestly excessive/repetitive requests, with documented justification.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 12 — Transparent information and modalities for rights. Controllers must respond to rights requests within one month, generally free of charge, using clear and plain language, and via the same channel the request came through.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Where data subjects submit a request electronically, we respond electronically unless otherwise requested.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Ensure the rights-request workflow defaults to matching the channel the request came in on.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 12 — Transparent information and modalities for rights. Controllers must respond to rights requests within one month, generally free of charge, using clear and plain language, and via the same channel the request came through.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Processors only process personal data on our documented instructions and don't engage sub-processors without our authorization.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Add a clause requiring processors to act only on documented instructions and seek authorization before adding sub-processors.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 29 — Processing under the authority of the controller or processor. Anyone with access to personal data acting under the controller's or processor's authority may only process it on instructions, unless required to do so by law.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We cooperate with our supervisory authority on request, including providing information needed for it to perform its tasks.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Assign an owner responsible for coordinating supervisory authority requests and cooperation.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 31 — Cooperation with the supervisory authority. Controllers and processors must cooperate with the supervisory authority in the performance of its tasks, on request.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We consult our supervisory authority before processing where a DPIA indicates high risk that can't be sufficiently mitigated.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a trigger so unmitigated high-risk DPIA findings escalate to prior consultation with the supervisory authority.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 36 — Prior consultation. If a DPIA indicates high risk that can't be sufficiently mitigated, the controller must consult the supervisory authority before starting that processing.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We've evaluated whether adherence to an approved code of conduct or certification mechanism could help demonstrate compliance.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Evaluate relevant sector codes of conduct or certification schemes (e.g. ISO 27701) to help demonstrate GDPR compliance.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Articles 40–43 — Codes of conduct and certification. Organisations can adopt approved sector codes of conduct or certification schemes to help demonstrate compliance, though these are voluntary, not mandatory.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Security measures account for the state of the art, cost of implementation, and the nature/scope/context/purposes of processing, not a one-size-fits-all baseline.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Document how your security measures were scaled to your specific risk profile, not just copied from a generic checklist.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 32 — Security of processing. Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures — considering the state of the art, cost, and risk — to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Any person acting under the authority of the controller or processor who has access to personal data only processes it on instructions.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Add an access-and-instructions clause to employee/contractor agreements covering personal data handling.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 29 — Processing under the authority of the controller or processor. Anyone with access to personal data acting under the controller's or processor's authority may only process it on instructions, unless required to do so by law.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Our breach notification to the supervisory authority includes the nature of the breach, categories/approximate number of individuals affected, and likely consequences.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a notification template that captures all Art 33(3) required elements, not just a summary.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 33 — Notification of a breach to the supervisory authority. A personal data breach must be notified to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of it, unless unlikely to result in risk to individuals — including specific required content and phased reporting where full details aren't yet available.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Where notification can't be made all at once, we provide information in phases without further undue delay.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Allow your breach notification workflow to submit initial info within 72 hours and supplement it as the investigation continues.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 33 — Notification of a breach to the supervisory authority. A personal data breach must be notified to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of it, unless unlikely to result in risk to individuals — including specific required content and phased reporting where full details aren't yet available.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We assess breach risk to determine whether individual notification is required, based on likelihood of high risk to their rights.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Define objective high-risk criteria in advance to decide when individual notification under Art 34 is triggered.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 34 — Communication of a breach to the data subject. Where a breach is likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, the controller must notify those individuals directly, without undue delay.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Awareness of potential administrative fines (up to €20m or 4% of global turnover) is factored into our incident risk planning.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Add GDPR fine exposure as a line item in your enterprise risk register alongside operational impact.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 83 — General conditions for administrative fines. Fines can reach up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher) for the most serious infringements.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: We maintain a documented DPIA screening process to determine when a full DPIA is required, not just for obviously high-risk projects.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Build a short DPIA screening questionnaire triggered at the start of every new processing project.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 35 — Data protection impact assessment (DPIA). Where processing is likely to result in high risk, the controller must carry out a DPIA before starting, assessing the risk and mitigation measures.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Our DPO has the necessary resources and access to information to perform their tasks effectively.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Ensure the DPO has dedicated time, budget, and system access, not just a title.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 38 — Position of the DPO. The DPO must be involved properly and in a timely manner in all data protection matters, given the resources needed to perform their tasks, and act independently — reporting to the highest level of management.

QUESTIONS_GDPR.text: Data subjects can contact our DPO directly on all issues related to the processing of their personal data.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.fix: Publish a direct DPO contact channel visible to data subjects, separate from general support.
QUESTIONS_GDPR.explain: Article 38 — Position of the DPO. The DPO must be involved properly and in a timely manner in all data protection matters, given the resources needed to perform their tasks, and act independently — reporting to the highest level of management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have confirmed whether our organization qualifies as an 'essential' or 'important' entity under NIS2 and registered accordingly with our competent authority.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Run a scoping assessment against NIS2 sector and size thresholds, and register with your national competent authority if in scope.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 3 & Annexes I–II — Scope. NIS2 applies to 'essential' and 'important' entities across listed critical sectors (energy, transport, banking, health, digital infrastructure, public administration, etc.), generally those above a medium-size threshold (50+ staff or €10m+ turnover).

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Our management body formally approves and oversees our cybersecurity risk-management measures.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Put board-level approval and periodic review of cybersecurity risk measures on the governance calendar.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 20(1) — Management body approval and liability. The management body must approve the entity's cybersecurity risk-management measures, oversee their implementation, and can be held personally liable for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Members of our management body undergo cybersecurity training to understand risks and assess risk-management practices.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Enroll management body members in cybersecurity training tailored to their oversight responsibilities.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 20(2) — Management body training. Members of the management body must undergo training to gain sufficient knowledge to identify and assess cybersecurity risks and evaluate management practices.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We provide cybersecurity awareness and hygiene training to employees broadly, not just IT/security staff.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Roll out basic cyber hygiene training to all employees, not just technical teams.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(g) — Measure 7 of 10: basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training for staff.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of our cybersecurity risk-management measures.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Define metrics and a review cadence to test whether your security measures are actually working.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(f) — Measure 6 of 10: policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We understand the personal liability management bodies can face for non-compliance, including potential restrictions on managerial roles.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Brief management on personal liability exposure under NIS2 so risk oversight is a board priority, not fully delegated.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 20(1) — Management body approval and liability. The management body must approve the entity's cybersecurity risk-management measures, oversee their implementation, and can be held personally liable for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have designated a specific person or team responsible for day-to-day NIS2 compliance.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Name an accountable owner for NIS2 compliance with a direct reporting line to management.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 20(1) — Management body approval and liability. The management body must approve the entity's cybersecurity risk-management measures, oversee their implementation, and can be held personally liable for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We maintain a risk register that is reviewed and updated on a regular schedule.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Maintain a living risk register reviewed at a fixed cadence, not just after incidents.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(1) — The core risk-management duty. Entities must take appropriate technical, operational, and organisational measures to manage cybersecurity risk, proportionate to their size, exposure, and the severity of potential incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Our cybersecurity strategy and policies are formally approved and periodically reviewed at board/senior leadership level.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Put annual cybersecurity strategy review and sign-off on the board's calendar.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 20(1) — Management body approval and liability. The management body must approve the entity's cybersecurity risk-management measures, oversee their implementation, and can be held personally liable for non-compliance.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We allocate budget and resources for cybersecurity that are proportionate to our assessed risk exposure.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Tie the cybersecurity budget explicitly to risk assessment findings, not a flat percentage guess.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(1) — The core risk-management duty. Entities must take appropriate technical, operational, and organisational measures to manage cybersecurity risk, proportionate to their size, exposure, and the severity of potential incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a documented policy on risk analysis and information system security, reviewed and updated regularly.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Draft and maintain a risk analysis and information security policy covering all in-scope systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(a) — Measure 1 of 10: policies on risk analysis and information system security.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We maintain an up-to-date inventory of hardware, software, and network assets supporting our critical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Build and maintain an asset inventory covering all systems that support essential/important services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(i) — Measure 9 of 10: human resources security, access control policies, and asset management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We enforce multi-factor authentication (or continuous authentication) for access to critical systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Roll out MFA across all admin, remote, and privileged access to critical systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(j) — Measure 10 of 10: the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication, secured voice/video/text communications, and secured emergency communication systems where appropriate.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have documented policies and procedures governing the use of cryptography and encryption.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Formalize a cryptography/encryption policy specifying approved algorithms and key management practices.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(h) — Measure 8 of 10: policies and procedures on the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a documented process for identifying, assessing, and remediating vulnerabilities in our systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Establish a vulnerability management program with defined remediation SLAs by severity.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(e) — Measure 5 of 10: security in the acquisition, development, and maintenance of network and information systems, including vulnerability handling and disclosure.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We maintain a coordinated vulnerability disclosure process allowing external researchers to report security issues responsibly.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Publish a vulnerability disclosure policy with a clear reporting channel and response commitment.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 12 — Coordinated vulnerability disclosure. Each member state designates a CSIRT to act as a coordinator for vulnerability disclosure, and entities are expected to support responsible external reporting of security flaws.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Security is built into our system and software acquisition, development, and maintenance lifecycle (secure SDLC).
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Embed security reviews and testing gates into your software development and procurement lifecycle.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(e) — Measure 5 of 10: security in the acquisition, development, and maintenance of network and information systems, including vulnerability handling and disclosure.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) or equivalent tooling across critical endpoints.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Deploy EDR or equivalent endpoint monitoring on systems supporting critical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(b) — Measure 2 of 10: incident handling — the processes for detecting, analysing, containing, and responding to security incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We maintain 24/7 (or appropriately staffed on-call) security monitoring capable of detecting incidents in near real time.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Establish round-the-clock monitoring or a tested on-call rotation for security alerts.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(b) — Measure 2 of 10: incident handling — the processes for detecting, analysing, containing, and responding to security incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Access to systems and data is governed by role-based access control tied to job function, reviewed periodically.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Implement role-based access control and schedule periodic access reviews.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(i) — Measure 9 of 10: human resources security, access control policies, and asset management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a documented incident response plan covering prevention, detection, analysis, containment, response, and recovery.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Document an incident response plan spanning the full lifecycle from prevention through recovery.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(b) — Measure 2 of 10: incident handling — the processes for detecting, analysing, containing, and responding to security incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have defined severity/classification criteria that determine when an incident is 'significant' and triggers reporting.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Define clear significance thresholds (operational disruption, financial loss, harm to others) mapped to reporting obligations.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(3) — What counts as 'significant.' An incident is significant if it has caused, or is capable of causing, severe operational disruption or financial loss, or has affected other persons through considerable material or non-material damage.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We can submit an early warning to our CSIRT or competent authority within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Build a 24-hour early-warning workflow and pre-approved template so the deadline is achievable under pressure.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(4)(a) — Early warning. Within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, an initial 'early warning' must be sent, indicating whether it's suspected to be caused by unlawful or malicious action.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We can submit a detailed incident notification, including severity assessment and indicators of compromise, within 72 hours.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Prepare a 72-hour notification template capturing severity, impact, and IOCs in advance.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(4)(b) — Incident notification. Within 72 hours, a fuller notification must follow, updating the early warning and providing an initial assessment including severity, impact, and indicators of compromise.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We can produce a final report, including root cause and mitigation measures, within one month of the incident notification.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Build a final-report template covering root cause, impact, and mitigation to hit the one-month deadline.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(4)(d) — Final report. Within one month of the incident notification, a final report is due, covering a detailed description, root cause, mitigation measures applied, and any cross-border impact.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We can provide an intermediate status report if requested by the CSIRT or competent authority during an ongoing incident.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Establish a process to produce ad hoc status updates on request during an active incident.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(4)(c) — Intermediate report. Upon request from the CSIRT or authority, the entity must provide a relevant status update while the incident is ongoing.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have identified our designated national CSIRT or competent authority and established contact before any incident occurs.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Establish contact with your national CSIRT/competent authority now, not during your first real incident.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(1) — The general notification duty. Essential and important entities must notify their CSIRT or competent authority of any incident with a significant impact on their services, without undue delay.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We conduct incident response tabletop exercises at least twice a year, involving executive management.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Run tabletop exercises at least twice yearly with participation from senior leadership.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(1) — The core risk-management duty. Entities must take appropriate technical, operational, and organisational measures to manage cybersecurity risk, proportionate to their size, exposure, and the severity of potential incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Post-incident reviews are conducted after significant incidents and findings are fed back into our risk register.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Formalize a post-incident review step that updates your risk register with lessons learned.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(f) — Measure 6 of 10: policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a coordinated workflow for incidents that trigger both NIS2 and other reporting obligations (e.g. GDPR) in parallel.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Build a single incident record with parallel reporting tracks so NIS2 and data-protection deadlines are handled together.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 23(6) — Where an incident also triggers other legal notification duties (e.g. under the GDPR), member states must ensure the two reporting obligations don't have to be handled as fully separate, disconnected processes.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We maintain business continuity plans, including tested backup and disaster recovery procedures, for critical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Document and test business continuity, backup, and disaster recovery plans for critical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(c) — Measure 3 of 10: business continuity, such as backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for critical systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Run a business impact analysis to set RTO/RPO targets for each critical system.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(c) — Measure 3 of 10: business continuity, such as backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a documented crisis management plan defining roles and decision-making authority during a major incident.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Draft a crisis management plan naming decision-makers and escalation paths for major incidents.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(c) — Measure 3 of 10: business continuity, such as backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We apply network segmentation to limit lateral movement between critical systems and general IT infrastructure.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Segment critical operational systems from general corporate networks to contain potential intrusions.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(a) — Measure 1 of 10: policies on risk analysis and information system security.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We conduct periodic penetration testing or red-team exercises against critical systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Schedule annual penetration testing or red-team exercises against systems supporting essential services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(e) — Measure 5 of 10: security in the acquisition, development, and maintenance of network and information systems, including vulnerability handling and disclosure.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Physical and environmental security controls protect the facilities hosting critical network and information systems.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Add physical access controls, environmental monitoring, and redundancy for critical system facilities.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(a) — Measure 1 of 10: policies on risk analysis and information system security.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have redundant, secure emergency communication channels for use if primary systems are compromised.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Establish an out-of-band emergency communication channel for crisis use.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(j) — Measure 10 of 10: the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication, secured voice/video/text communications, and secured emergency communication systems where appropriate.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We assess exposure to risk based on entity size, sector, and likely severity of incidents, and scale controls accordingly.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Formally document a risk assessment considering size, sector, and incident likelihood/severity to justify your control set.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(1) — The core risk-management duty. Entities must take appropriate technical, operational, and organisational measures to manage cybersecurity risk, proportionate to their size, exposure, and the severity of potential incidents.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We conduct internal or external audits of our cybersecurity risk-management measures on a periodic basis.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Schedule periodic internal or third-party audits of your NIS2 controls ahead of any regulator-ordered audit.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(f) — Measure 6 of 10: policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have considered using European cybersecurity certification schemes for relevant ICT products, services, or processes.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Evaluate whether adopting an EU cybersecurity certification scheme would streamline your compliance evidence.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 24 — Use of European cybersecurity certification schemes. Member states can require, and entities can voluntarily use, EU-recognised certification schemes to help demonstrate compliance with Article 21.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We assess the cybersecurity posture of direct suppliers and service providers before onboarding them.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Add a cybersecurity assessment step to supplier onboarding, focused on direct suppliers and service providers.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We evaluate the overall quality and resilience of products and services provided by our suppliers.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Score suppliers on product/service quality and resilience as part of vendor risk assessment.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We assess whether our suppliers follow secure development practices for the products/services they provide us.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Request evidence of secure development practices from suppliers providing software or technical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Vendor contracts require suppliers to notify us of security incidents quickly enough to meet our own 24-hour reporting window.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Add a supplier incident-notification clause with a deadline tight enough to preserve your own 24-hour NIS2 window.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We continuously monitor the cybersecurity posture of critical ICT suppliers, not just at onboarding.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Set up ongoing monitoring (not one-time due diligence) for suppliers critical to your operations.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have visibility into significant fourth-party/sub-supplier relationships that could affect our critical services.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Require critical suppliers to disclose their own significant sub-suppliers.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We review supplier security certifications (e.g. ISO 27001, SOC 2) or audit reports on a periodic basis.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Request and review supplier security certifications or audit reports at least annually.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: Vendor contracts define clear security requirements and consequences for non-compliance.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Add explicit security SLAs and remedies for non-compliance to supplier contracts.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We are aware of and monitor any EU Cooperation Group coordinated risk assessments relevant to our critical supply chains (e.g. 5G, cloud).
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Track EU Cooperation Group sector-specific supply chain risk assessments relevant to your critical suppliers.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 22 — Union-level coordinated risk assessments. The EU Cooperation Group can carry out coordinated security risk assessments of specific critical supply chains (e.g. 5G networks) that entities in those chains need to stay aware of.

QUESTIONS_NIS2.text: We have a secure offboarding process for vendors, including revoking access and confirming data/asset return or destruction.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.fix: Formalize vendor offboarding to revoke access and confirm return/destruction of shared assets or data.
QUESTIONS_NIS2.explain: Article 21(2)(d) — Measure 4 of 10: supply chain security, including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We give an itemised notice, in clear and plain language, describing the personal data collected and the purpose of processing, before or at the time of seeking consent.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Publish an itemised notice shown before or at the point of consent.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 5 — Notice. Before or at the time of seeking consent, the Data Fiduciary must give an itemised notice describing the personal data collected and the purpose, in clear language the principal can understand.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Consent requests are presented separately from other unrelated terms and conditions, not bundled in.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Separate the consent request from general terms of service into its own clear step.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 6(1) — A consent request must be presented in clear and plain language, and separately from any other unrelated information — no bundling or pre-ticked boxes.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We implement reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breaches.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Implement encryption, access controls, and monitoring proportionate to the data processed.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 8(5) — The Data Fiduciary must implement reasonable security safeguards to prevent a personal data breach.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Personal data is retained only as long as necessary for the stated purpose or as required by law, and is erased afterward.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Define retention schedules per data category and automate deletion once the purpose or legal requirement ends.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 8(7)–(8) — Personal data must be erased once the specified purpose is no longer being served and retention isn't required by any other law, subject to any prescribed retention periods.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We obtain verifiable parental or guardian consent before processing a child's personal data.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Implement age-gating and a verifiable parental consent workflow for any user under 18.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 9 — Processing of children's/persons-with-disabilities' data. Before processing a child's data, verifiable parental or lawful guardian consent is required, and the fiduciary must not process data in a way likely to cause detriment to the child's wellbeing, nor undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We have a documented process for correction, completion, updating, and erasure of personal data on request.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Set up an intake form and SLA tracker for correction/erasure requests.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 12 — Right to correction and erasure. A data principal can request correction of inaccurate/misleading data, completion of incomplete data, updating, and erasure of data no longer necessary for its purpose.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We have identified and documented a lawful basis for every processing purpose.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Build a data-processing inventory tagging each purpose with its lawful basis.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 6 — Lawfulness of processing. Every processing activity needs one of six lawful bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Special category data (health, biometric, sexual orientation, etc.) is processed only under one of the specific Article 9(2) conditions, with extra safeguards.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Tag special category data separately and document the specific Art 9(2) condition relied on for each use case.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 9 — Special categories of data. Processing data revealing racial/ethnic origin, health, biometric, genetic, sexual orientation, or similar sensitive attributes is prohibited unless a specific Article 9(2) exception applies.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Personal data is minimized, kept accurate, and not retained longer than necessary for its purpose.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Review data fields for necessity, add accuracy checks, and define retention limits per category.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 5(1)(c) — Data minimisation. Personal data collected must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what's necessary for the purpose — not gathered 'just in case.'

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Privacy by design and by default is built into new systems and products from the start, not bolted on afterward.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Add a privacy-by-design checklist to the product development lifecycle.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 25 — Data protection by design and by default. Privacy safeguards must be built into systems from the design stage, and default settings must be the most privacy-protective option.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Where automated decision-making or profiling produces legal or similarly significant effects, we provide safeguards and a path to human review.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Disclose automated decision logic and offer a clear path to request human review.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 22 — Automated individual decision-making. Individuals have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing (including profiling) that has legal or similarly significant effects, with limited exceptions and required safeguards including human review.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We process employee/HR data under a specific lawful basis and any applicable collective agreement or national employment-context rules.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Document the specific lawful basis and any collective agreement governing HR/employee data processing.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 88 — Processing in the employment context. Member states can adopt more specific rules for processing employees' data, which may include collective agreements — so HR data processing may have extra national/local requirements layered on top of the GDPR.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We have a process to give data principals a summary of their personal data and the processing activities carried out on it, on request.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Build a self-serve or manual process to generate a data summary report on request.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 11 — Right to access information. A data principal can obtain a summary of their personal data being processed and the processing activities carried out.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We support nomination of another individual to exercise a data principal's rights in the event of death or incapacity.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Add a nominee-designation field and a documented handling process for nominee requests.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 14 — Right to nominate. A data principal can nominate another individual to exercise their rights in the event of death or incapacity.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: Data principals are made aware of their duties under the Act (providing accurate information, not filing false or frivolous grievances).
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Add a short 'your responsibilities' section alongside the privacy notice.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Section 15 — Duties of data principals. Data principals also have duties — not to impersonate, not to suppress material information, not to register false or frivolous grievances, and to provide only authentic information.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We respond to access, rectification, erasure, and restriction requests within the statutory timeline.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Set up an intake form and SLA tracker guaranteeing a one-month response window.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 12 — Transparent information and modalities for rights. Controllers must respond to rights requests within one month, generally free of charge, using clear and plain language, and via the same channel the request came through.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We support the right to data portability in a structured, commonly-used, machine-readable format.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Add a machine-readable data export option (e.g. JSON/CSV) to account settings.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 20 — Right to data portability. Individuals can receive their data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and have it transmitted to another controller where technically feasible.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: When a data subject objects to processing, especially for direct marketing, we stop that processing immediately.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Build a marketing-objection flag that immediately suppresses further marketing processing.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 21 — Right to object. Individuals can object to processing based on legitimate interests or public task at any time; for direct marketing, the objection must be honoured immediately and unconditionally.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We can articulate a specific ground before erasing data on request, rather than deleting reflexively.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Map every erasure request to a specific Art 17(1) ground before acting on or refusing it.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 17 — Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). Individuals can request deletion of their data on specific grounds (e.g. purpose fulfilled, consent withdrawn, unlawful processing) — it isn't an unconditional right, so the controller must map the request to a specific ground.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We provide rights-request responses free of charge, except for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Default to free rights-request handling; reserve fees only for manifestly excessive requests.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 12 — Transparent information and modalities for rights. Controllers must respond to rights requests within one month, generally free of charge, using clear and plain language, and via the same channel the request came through.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We use pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Enforce TLS in transit and AES-256 at rest; pseudonymize data in lower environments.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 32(1)(a) — One of the specific measures Article 32 calls out: pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We can ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Deploy access controls, monitoring, and redundancy to protect system CIA properties.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 32(1)(b) — Another specific Article 32 measure: the ability to ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We can restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Schedule an annual restore drill to confirm backups and recovery actually work.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 32(1)(c) — Another specific Article 32 measure: the ability to restore availability and access to data in a timely manner after a physical or technical incident.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We regularly test, assess, and evaluate the effectiveness of our technical and organizational security measures.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Schedule quarterly vulnerability scans and an annual penetration test.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 32(1)(d) — Another specific Article 32 measure: a process for regularly testing, assessing, and evaluating the effectiveness of security measures.

QUESTIONS_ALL.text: We have a documented policy on risk analysis and information system security, reviewed and updated regularly.
QUESTIONS_ALL.fix: Draft and maintain a risk analysis and information security policy covering all in-scope systems.
QUESTIONS_ALL.explain: Article 21(2)(a) — Measure 1 of 10: policies on risk analysis and information system security.
